DEPARTMENT OF FOREIGN Page No.: Page 1 of 1 AFFAIRS HOME OFFICE OPERATIONS MANUAL Revision No.: 00 ) INTRODUCTION ON THE ot 3 November DFA-OM DEPARTMENT Effectivity: 2015 The Department of Foreign Affairs (DFA) had its auspicious beginnings when President Emilio Aguinaldo appointed Apolinario Mabini as the Republic's first Secretary of Foreign Affairs on 23 June 1898. The DFA was among the first government departments created following the establishment of the First Philippine Republic. The Philippines underwent colonial rule under the United States from 1898 to 1946, and Japanese occupation f

ippines underwent colonial rule under the United States from 1898 to 1946, and Japanese occupation from 1942 to 1944. The country regained independence, including full control of foreign affairs and diplomatic matters, on 4 July 1946. Commonwealth Act No. 732 was passed creating the Department of Foreign Affairs. Shortly thereafter, President Manuel Roxas issued on 16 September 1946, Executive Order No. 18 providing for the organization and operation of the DFA and the Foreign Service. The main tasks of the DFA then were to assist in post-war rehabilitation, formulate policies for investment p

tasks of the DFA then were to assist in post-war rehabilitation, formulate policies for investment promotion, and establish diplomatic relations with other countries. The DFA's role and responsibilities have evolved over the years. Most Filipinos presently associate the DFA with the issuance of passports and the extension of authentication services both here and in its network abroad of 60 embassies, 21 consulates general and 4 missions to international organizations. In order to make consular services more accessible to the Filipino public, the DFA has established around 21 regional consular

ervices more accessible to the Filipino public, the DFA has established around 21 regional consular offices in provinces and 5 satellite offices in the National Capital Region (NCR), as of November 2015. More significantly, the DFA is entrusted with crafting, coordinating and implementing Philippine foreign policy to advance the interests of the Philippines and the Filipino people in the world community. It discharges its duties through the following fundamental policy pillars: 1. Contribution to the preservation and enhancement of national security, including the protection of territorial int

o the preservation and enhancement of national security, including the protection of territorial integrity and national sovereignty; 2. Promotion of economic security, while ensuring inclusive growth and sustainable development; and 3. Protection of the rights and promotion of the welfare and interest of Filipinos overseas, recognizing them as partners in national development.

INTRODUCTION TO THE MANUAL The Department of Foreign Affairs, in accordance with Republic Act No. 9485, also known as the Anti-Red Tape Act of 2007 enacted Department Order No. 01-12, or the Institution of Operations Manual at all Levels and Units of the Department. DO 01-12 was created to streamline the operations in all units of the Department and increase transparency and accountability among offices. It aims to enhance pe

of the Department and increase transparency and accountability among offices. It aims to enhance personnel awareness on their respective duties and responsibilities thereby increasing work output and productivity. DO 01-12 is applicable to all units within the Home Office, Office of Consular Affairs, Satellite Offices, Regional Consular Offices, and Foreign Service Posts. Unit Operations Manuals are regularly updated by the Process Owners who are usually the heads of the office/ unit concerned. B. SCOPE AND EXCLUSION This Manual is an overview of the Department of Foreign Affairs’ (DFA) opera

B. SCOPE AND EXCLUSION This Manual is an overview of the Department of Foreign Affairs’ (DFA) operations, powers of authority, duties and responsibilities, and basic work procedures, specifically in its headquarters located in Pasay City often referred to as the DFA Home Office. This Operations Manual adheres to the DFA’s mandate enshrined in Republic Act No. 7157, also known as the Philippine Foreign Service Act of 1991, as well as, to existing national government policies, international agreements, and DFA rules and regulations that govern its daily operations. The DFA ensures that its work

ments, and DFA rules and regulations that govern its daily operations. The DFA ensures that its work products, services, and decisions respond to the requirements of its internal and external clients, and that resources and processes are aligned with DFA’s strategic directions. The DFA plans, implements, documents, and assesses work activities for continual improvement. Focus shall be provided in detailing the process of the Authentication Division at the DFA Office of Consular Affairs in Aseana, Parafaque City (OCA DFA-Aseana) only. The DFA authentication service is critical to the Department

, Parafaque City (OCA DFA-Aseana) only. The DFA authentication service is critical to the Department's function in view of the increasing demand for internationally recognized documents brought about by globalization. This Operations Manual will not cover the authentication process in Philippine Foreign Service Posts (FSPs), DFA Regional Consular Offices (RCOs), and DFA Satellite Offices (SOs).

SYSTEM OF AMENDMENT, REVISION, AND DISTRIBUTION C.1. AMENDMENT/ REVISION The Office of the Undersecretary for Administration (OUA) initiates the amendment/ revision of this Operations Manual (DFA-OM). A draft of the proposed amendment/ revision in the DFA-OM is submitted, for approval, to the Undersecretary who affixes his/ her approval on the routing slip. The soft copy of the DFA-OM is maintained by OUA. Amendments and revision

roval on the routing slip. The soft copy of the DFA-OM is maintained by OUA. Amendments and revisions are done when there are changes in policies and regulations relevant to the DFA C.2. DESCRIPTION OF UNIT’S MANDATE AND WORK PLAN The Department of Foreign Affairs performs its authentication function and services in the context of the 1963 Vienna Convention on Consular Relations. Given the existence of a multi-state system, the

f the 1963 Vienna Convention on Consular Relations. Given the existence of a multi-state system, the said convention stipulates that, among other things, the various documents sourced from a particular sovereign state, i.e. public documents, private documents and commercial documents shall have validity or operative effect in another sovereign state’s legal jurisdiction after having been subjected to an agreed-upon authentication procedure. The DFA through the Authentication Division — Office of Consular Affairs may authenticate an act, deed, document, etc. executed or sourced within Philippin

Consular Affairs may authenticate an act, deed, document, etc. executed or sourced within Philippine legal jurisdiction by way of certifying said act. When these acts, deeds or documents have been duly authenticated by the Department, the receiving Embassies or Consulates or any other foreign legal entities are, in effect assured that aforesaid documents are in order or have been legalized in accordance with proper procedure. Foreign Embassies, Consulates and foreign legal entities may or may not require the authentication of documents sourced from the Philippines for use within their respect
ot require the authentication of documents sourced from the Philippines for use within their respective jurisdictions. However, under rules of reciprocity in international relations,

s’ inquiries regarding their requirements. The Unit also acts as the “1%t line of defence” in responding to applicants’ comments, suggestions, grievances, and complaints. The procedures for filing application for Authentication are as follows: Step 1: Applicant fills out an application form (maximum of 5 documents per application form). Step 2: Applicant presents a valid ID upon submission of the documents to the Processing Window. Step 3: Applicant pays appropriate Authentication Fee to the Cashier (as scheduled below). have legal force and effect in the Philippines. Section 632 of said Department Order provides “When an officer in a foreign country other than an officer of the Philippines has performed a notarial or other official act for use in the Philippines, the official character of the executing officer should be verified by the diplomatic or consular officer of the Philippines. Revised Rules of Court of the Philippines The Philippines Rules of Court require that foreign documents to be admissible in Philippine courts should be properly authenticated by the Philippine embassy / consulate officials concerned. Section 24 of Rule 132 (Proof of Official Record), provides that “the record of public documents ... when admissible for any purpose, may be evidenced by an official publication thereof or by a copy attested by the officer having the legal custody of the record, or by his deputy, and accompanied, if the record is not kept in the P

e legal custody of the record, or by his deputy, and accompanied, if the record is not kept in the Philippines, with a certificate that such officer has the custody. If the office in which the record is kept is in a foreign country, the certificate may be made by a secretary of the embassy or legation, consul general, consul, vice-consul or consular agent or by any officer in the foreign service of the Philippines stationed in the foreign country in which the record is kept, and authenticated by the seal of his office”. It goes without saying that the act of verification of relevant documents by the Philippine labor officials abroad may be dispensed with but not the act of authenti

documents by the Philippine labor officials abroad may be dispensed with but not the act of authentication of said documents by the Philippine embassy / consulate officials which is mandated by the above-mentioned provisions of laws, rules and regulations. Furthermore, the verification by Labor Attaches and the authentication by diplomatic or consular officials have different purposes. The former verifies the contents of the labor contracts and assures that it is in accordance with Philippine labor laws. The latter makes the contract an official document so that it can be admissible in Philipp

bor laws. The latter makes the contract an official document so that it can be admissible in Philippine courts and/or by other government offices and other private entities either for public or private purposes. Page | 31

CO AINTS, FEEDBACK MECHANISMS AND EMERGEN SITUATION Complaints The verbal complaint from an applicant is acted upon by the Supervisor assigned in Processing Unit/ Pending and Correction Unit/ Releasing Unit. If the Unit Supervisor cannot resolve the complaint, this shall be elevated to the Frontline Operations Section Supervisor. If the Frontline Operations Section Supervisor cannot resolve the complaint, this shall be elevated to the Assistant Director. If the Assistant Dir

cannot resolve the complaint, this shall be elevated to the Assistant Director. If the Assistant Director cannot resolve the complaint, this shall be elevated to the Director. The written complaint from an applicant through the client feedback form, letters, communications from government agency, etc. is forwarded to Administration Section for the Director's perusal. The Director will instruct the concerned Supervisor for immediate action and resolution. aged to fill up the Client Feedback Form to express their appreciation, suggestion or grievances in Authentication Service for the Division’s consideration and action. Regular dialogue meetings are held with Partner Issuing Government Agencies for the improvement of Authentication Services. Staff meetings are also held regularly to ensure efficient service to the public. Emergency Situation In case of emergency situations, everyone must be informed and stay alert for further instructions. The assigned Security Officer of the Division, through coordination with Office of Intelligence and Securi

signed Security Officer of the Division, through coordination with Office of Intelligence and Security (OIS), takes charge for concerted action to be taken by the various offices in response to the emergency situation.
